summary: This notebook serves as the data preparation stage for a YOLO-based object detection pipeline targeting bacterial flagellar motors in tomographic data. It demonstrates a robust preprocessing workflow that extracts 2D slices around annotated motor centers, applies percentile-based intensity normalization, and structures the output into a YOLO-compatible format. A key takeaway is the importance of performing train/validation splits at the tomogram level rather than the slice level to prevent data leakage. The notebook also highlights practical considerations like arbitrary bounding box sizing and the need for a standardized dataset configuration file for downstream training.
competition_flow: Loads tomographic slices and annotations → extracts and normalizes 2D slices around motor locations → splits data at the tomogram level → outputs a YOLO-compatible dataset with bounding boxes and configuration file.
data_reading: Reads motor location annotations from train_labels.csv using pandas
Loads 2D tomographic slices as JPEG images using PIL from nested directory paths
data_processing: Applies percentile-based contrast enhancement (clipping to 2nd/98th percentiles and scaling to [0, 255])
Performs a strict tomogram-level 80/20 train/validation split
Normalizes bounding box coordinates to [0, 1] for YOLO format
cv_strategy: Tomogram-level holdout split (80/20) to prevent data leakage.
insights: Splitting data at the tomogram level rather than the slice level is critical to prevent data leakage in tomographic datasets.
Percentile-based contrast normalization effectively standardizes slice intensities across varying tomographic conditions.
YOLO training requires bounding boxes normalized to image dimensions and a standardized YAML configuration file.
